title: Ollama
lesson:
Ollama is the easiest way to run a language model on your own computer. It is a small program that downloads a model, loads it, and gives you a chat prompt and a tiny web server — all from one command. If llama.cpp is the powerful engine, Ollama is the whole car wrapped around it: you do not think about pistons, you just turn the key and drive.

The Docker-for-models idea

The clearest analogy is Docker, the tool developers use to run apps in tidy, self-contained boxes called containers. Docker made it normal to type docker run nginx and have a web server appear in seconds, no manual setup. Ollama copies that feel for AI. You type ollama run llama3.2 and a chat-ready model appears in seconds. It pulls the model from a registry (an online library of ready-to-use models), stores it locally, and runs it — the same one-command rhythm Docker fans already love.

# Download (if needed) and start chatting
ollama run llama3.2

# List models you have on disk
ollama list

# Remove one to free space
ollama rm llama3.2

What Ollama actually does for you

Under the hood Ollama uses llama.cpp to do the heavy lifting and GGUF files (the single-file model format built for laptops) as its model packaging. But it hides all of that. Three things make it beginner-friendly:

  • It picks sensible defaults. It chooses a good quantization level — quantization means storing each number with fewer bits to save memory, like vacuum-packing clothes into a smaller suitcase — so you do not have to decode cryptic file names like Q4_K_M.
  • It manages memory and hardware. It detects your GPU (the chip that does fast parallel math) or falls back to your CPU, and it loads and unloads models so they do not eat all your VRAM (the fast memory on a graphics card).
  • It runs a local API. Quietly it starts a web server on your machine at localhost:11434, so your own code or apps can talk to the model. This is local inference: the model runs on your hardware, your data never leaves the room.

Talking to it from code

Because Ollama exposes an HTTP server, any program can send it prompts. Newer Ollama versions even speak the same request shape as the OpenAI API, so swapping a cloud model for a local one can be a one-line change — handy when building chatbots you want to run for free and offline.

import requests

r = requests.post("http://localhost:11434/api/generate", json={
    "model": "llama3.2",
    "prompt": "Explain photosynthesis to a 7-year-old.",
    "stream": False
})
print(r.json()["response"])

The Modelfile: your own custom model

Ollama has its own recipe file called a Modelfile. Think of it like a Dockerfile, or like a coffee order card pinned to the machine: it says which base model to start from, what system prompt (the standing instruction that shapes the model's personality) to bake in, and what settings to use, like temperature (how creative versus predictable the answers are). You then build a named model from it once, and run it like any other.

FROM llama3.2
SYSTEM "You are a terse pirate. Answer in one short sentence."
PARAMETER temperature 0.8

ollama create pirate -f Modelfile
ollama run pirate

This is also how you load a model you fine-tuned: convert it to GGUF (perhaps after training a LoRA adapter), point a Modelfile's FROM at the .gguf file, and build. Your custom behaviour now runs with the same one-command convenience.

Where Ollama fits — and where it does not

Ollama is built for one person on one machine: a developer prototyping, a privacy-conscious app, a hobbyist on a laptop or Mac. It is wonderfully simple but not built to serve thousands of users at once. For that you want a high-throughput server like vLLM, which is the opposite trade-off: harder to set up, far faster under heavy load.

OllamavLLM
One command, runs anywhereServer setup, GPU required
Great for one user / dev / privacyBuilt for many users at once
CPU or modest GPU is fineWants a strong data-center GPU
Auto-loads/unloads modelsKeeps a model pinned for speed

💡 If your goal is "run a good model on my laptop tonight without reading a manual," Ollama is the answer. Start with a small model that fits your memory, then size up.
mentalModel: Ollama is Docker for language models: one command pulls, packs, and runs a model on your own machine — it wraps the llama.cpp engine and GGUF files, picks sensible defaults, manages your hardware, and quietly serves a local API, so you just turn the key and drive.
mistakes: Picking a model far bigger than your memory. A 70B model will not fit on a typical laptop. If it runs at all it spills onto disk and crawls. Match the model to your RAM/VRAM — start with a 3B–8B model and only size up if it fits comfortably.

Assuming Ollama is multi-user and production-grade. It serves requests roughly one at a time by default. Under real concurrent traffic it slows badly. For many simultaneous users, reach for vLLM or another serving stack instead.

Confusing Ollama with the model itself. Ollama is the runner, not the brain. llama3.2, mistral, and qwen are the actual models you load into it. Saying "Ollama is slow" usually means the model you chose is too big, not the tool.

Forgetting it keeps running in the background. Ollama leaves a server listening on localhost:11434 and may keep a model loaded in memory. If your machine feels low on RAM, check ollama ps and unload models you are not using.

Editing a Modelfile and expecting the change to apply automatically. A Modelfile is a build recipe. You must re-run ollama create after changing it; just editing the file does nothing to an already-built model.

Trusting the default context length for long documents. Ollama may cap the context window (how much text the model can read at once) lower than the model supports, silently truncating long inputs. Set num_ctx explicitly when you feed it big files.
exercise:
Install Ollama and run ollama run llama3.2 (or any small model that fits your machine). Chat with it, then open a second terminal and hit its API directly with curl or the Python snippet above, asking the same question. Confirm you get the same kind of answer from both the chat prompt and your code. Finally, run ollama list and ollama ps to see what is on disk and what is loaded in memory.

Stretch goal: Write a Modelfile that turns your base model into a tightly-scoped assistant — for example a SQL helper with a system prompt and a low temperature — build it with ollama create, and call your custom model from a 20-line script. Bonus: point Ollama at the OpenAI-compatible endpoint and swap it into an app you already wrote against a cloud model.
solution: Hint: The chat REPL and the API are the same engine, so answers should match in style (wording varies because generation is random unless you fix the seed). If curl fails to connect, the background server is not running — start it with ollama serve or just launch the app. For the Modelfile, the three lines that matter are FROM (which base model), SYSTEM (the standing instruction), and PARAMETER temperature 0.2 (low = focused and predictable, good for SQL). Remember to re-run ollama create mysql-helper -f Modelfile every time you tweak it. For the OpenAI swap, point the client's base URL at http://localhost:11434/v1 and use any string as the API key.
summary: Ollama is the simplest on-ramp to running models locally: one command pulls and runs a model, wrapping llama.cpp and GGUF behind sensible defaults, hardware management, and a built-in local API. Use a Modelfile to bake in a system prompt, settings, or your own fine-tuned GGUF. It is perfect for a single developer, privacy, or offline use — but for serving many users at once, switch to a heavier server like vLLM.

keyTerms.def: A program that downloads, packages, and runs language models locally with one command, wrapping the llama.cpp engine and exposing a local API — Docker-like simplicity for AI.
Ollama's online library of ready-to-run models. Typing ollama run <name> pulls the model from it the first time, then reuses the local copy.
Ollama's recipe file (like a Dockerfile) declaring a base model, a system prompt, and settings. You build a named custom model from it with 'ollama create'.
The web server Ollama runs at localhost:11434 so your own code and apps can send prompts to the model on your machine. Newer versions also offer an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
A setting from roughly 0 to 1+ controlling randomness: low values give focused, predictable answers; high values give more creative, varied ones.
The Ollama parameter that sets the context window — how much text the model can read at once. The default may be smaller than the model supports, silently cutting long inputs.
Storing each model number with fewer bits to shrink memory use, like vacuum-packing clothes. Ollama picks a sensible quantization level for you automatically.
The command that shows which models Ollama currently has loaded in memory, so you can spot and unload ones you are not using to free RAM or VRAM.
